items.title: Fit encoders on training data only
items.avoid: Calling `fit` on concatenated train+validation+test or computing target means before the train/validation split.
items.prefer: Fit `OneHotEncoder`, ordinal maps, and target statistics on `X_train` (or inner CV folds); persist and apply the same artifact at inference.
items.why: Holdout labels and category distributions must not influence preprocessing; otherwise metrics are optimistically biased.

items.title: Match encoding to variable type
items.avoid: Defaulting every string column to `LabelEncoder` integers.
items.prefer: One-hot for nominal; explicit ordered maps for ordinal; target encoding only when cardinality is high and CV smoothing is in place.
items.why: Encoding choice is a modeling assumption, not a convenience step.
items.example: `education`: HS < Bach < MS < PhD → ordinal 0–3; `browser`: Chrome/Firefox/Safari → one-hot.

items.title: Define unseen-category policy before production
items.avoid: Assuming training vocabulary covers all future labels.
items.prefer: `handle_unknown='ignore'`, an explicit `__unknown__` level, or rare-level bucketing learned at train time.
items.why: New merchants, cities, or product SKUs appear continuously; silent all-zero vectors change model behavior unpredictably.
items.example: Map any provider not in `enc.categories_[0]` to the `other` column added during training bucketing.

items.title: Smooth target encodings for rare levels
items.avoid: Raw per-category means with no count threshold on sparse groups.
items.prefer: Blend category mean with global mean: `(n * cat_mean + m * global_mean) / (n + m)` for small n.
items.why: Rare categories otherwise encode noise and dominate overfitting on leaderboard splits.
items.example: Zip codes with < 5 sales use m=10 prior toward national median price before entering the feature matrix.
